replies.author: @merakimelodies8931
replies.text:
Allow me to enter the conversation: I am a life-long martial-arts enthusiast-and-historian and a practitioner of both Muay-Thai and a version of KungFu that is not dance but actually does body-conditioning and sparring, including practicing swordsmanship with a jian and dao against resisting opponents (with proper protective-equipment), so I know a thing or two.

*Count Juan Raphael Dante:*
Legitimate karate-blackbelt and verified-veteran was trained in handled firearms and explosives regularly, could genuinely fight, helped found the American Karate Association, promoted full-contact training, pioneered some of the first mixing of martial-arts in public-matches, is proven by records to have been a college-wrestler-and-boxer, had Chicago-mob connections, and is likely to have participated in one of the largest heists in history and gotten away clean; but also went a bit crazy after getting on drugs and sold voodoo-like bullshido-manuals to impressionable teenagers, so yeah, that messed up his legacy a good bit.

*Frank Dux:*
A martial-arts scammer who wrote a lot of ninja-novellas in diary-form and/or historical-format that he then passed off as legitimate, saying that they were autobiographical of himself and biographical of his alleged-teacher: Tenzo, “Tiger,” Tanaka (apparently, such a man is confirmed to have lived and died near where Dux lived, but no details about his life can be confirmed), which was oddly also the name of a man in the James Bond film that first introduced the word, “ninja,” and the modernized pop-culture-interpretation of the Shinobi-No-Mono and Shinobi-No-Jutsu (or, “Ninjutsu”) to Western audiences and helped spark the massive ninja-craze of the 1980’s and the 1990’s (Dux was fascinated by James Bond, comparing ninja and himself, as an alleged-ninja, to Bond on multiple talk-shows). Frank Dux would claim to have received the Medal of Honor, which he awarded to himself, as well as to have fought in Vietnam and in SouthEast-Asia as part of the special-operations team Mac-V-Sog (not possible, according to his service-records) and to have received awards for his knife-fighting skills (which the military does not offer), as well as to have gone A.W.O.L. to compete in a secret martial-arts d3@match called, “The Kumite,” which was invitation-only and said to take place in HongKong’s Kowloon Walled-City before it was eventually-demolished. Oddly enough, this deception got the movie, “BloodSport,” made, which went on to inspire the UFC, fighting-games such as Mortal-Kombat and Street-Fighter, and such martial-arts legends as Bas Ruten and Tito Ortiz while helping create the martial-arts craze of the 1980’s that can still be felt rippling through society to this day: Dux is a lovable scammer, at the end of the day, who almost single-handedly exploded the martial-arts crowd of the West, making his lie into a truth in many ways over time (the martial-arts world owes him a great debt, even if it cannot condone how he accomplished it: he made it happen and got the ball rolling).

*Ashida Kim:*
A crazy, middle-aged (possibly now elderly) Floridian version of a hobgoblin who L.A.R.P.’s as a ninja while promoting voodoo-magic, “martial-arts,” and who Dante’s group even disowns and tried to sue him over claiming to be a member of their organization; also known for publishing romantic-ninja novels that are flat-out creepy and weird.

*George Dillman:*
Another once-widely-respected martial-artist and high-ranking Karate-blackbelt who would hang out and train with Muhammad-Ali, and who was once considered the realest-deal around; but who, almost-inexplicably, appears to have lost his mind and started teaching, “no-touch-knockout,” utter-bullshido (perhaps displaying the most-famous instance of such when he was the subject of a documentary, had one of his students attempt to knock out a skeptic of his arts, and then said that the skeptic wasn’t knocked out either because he didn’t believe or because he had one toe lifted the wrong way and nullified the no-touch-knockout).

*Michael Fabia:*
A fraudulent parasite who passed himself off to a professional-fighter and slowly replaced said-fighter’s entire crew with just himself, then ruining said-fighter’s career and many aspects of his life. Fabia was a new-age, postmodern-progressive spiritualist-(alleged)-healer who taught space-time manipulation and interdimentional-travel and was a master-manipulator more akin to a cult-leader than an authentic trainer. The martial-artist he manipulated is not being named here, out of his desire to not be associated with Fabia any-longer and to respect his break-away from Fabia. Fabia is the worst.

*Michael Ryabko/Ryabco:*
A Systema instructor, Ryabko is considered to be a portly old-man who just lost his mind as he got old, eventually teaching bullshido of the highest order, such as no-touch-knockouts, while going around with a leather-whip to beat his students and giving off some major leather-daddy vibes.

*Steven Segall:*
Steven was a well-known action-star, but before that, he was an Aikido student in Japan. Originally, to the best of my awareness, Aikido was simply taught to be a gentle way of offbalancing, tripping, and joint-locking foes, and was a training-supplement to people who had already practiced other martial-arts (such as Traditional Japanese-Jiujitsu or Judo or Karate, for-example)—a martial-art for already-trained men to learn how to peacefully submit someone when grabbed or shoved, and to do so in a way that wouldn’t do major damage by hitting their foe with the earth, essentially, as judoka like to say (pretty epic way of putting it, to be honest). It was never meant to be an independent, sufficient, and complete martial-art; however, that is the direction it, unfortunately, went in. Steven trained in it, learned some legitimate techniques but mostly a lot of bullshido, and helped choreograph some katana-duels for Japanese-movies. He then moved to Hollywood, was picked up by a talent-scout who one of his flippy-floppy displays actually convinced, and landed rolls that saw massive success up-front. Eventually, harassment charges, marrying another woman while still married to his first wife, becoming a micro-manager on set and being very-hard to work with, breaking Sean Connery’s wrist and picking fights with stuntmen, and a failed one-movie-career as a director resulted in him being a Hollywood-crashout instead of a Hollywood-cashcow, and he has gained weight and been doing straight-to-DVD releases ever-since.

replies.author: @merakimelodies8931
replies.text: ⁠@christianfeliciano6079I’ve seen a few of those. Basically, it’s always someone talking about the double-bladed-sword incident (one incident does not a fraud or charlatan make), taking him out-of-context, putting words in his mouth, making fun of something he didn’t actually do or say, or just blathering about some political difference they had because they got their fragile-egos bruised by something he said. I’m not saying that’s all that exist: simply that’s all that I’ve seen, personally. A good example of this is the Fredda video, which both Shad and Metatron have done reply-videos to and utterly-dismantled post-haste. Plus, Shad has never claimed to be a martial-arts expert: he claims to be an enthusiast and a practitioner who can be in-error and encourages people to research for themselves instead of following him blindly (which, to be fair, even if he has made the rare mistake or two, he’s pretty-well exonerated himself by either proving himself right in replies or owning it and correcting himself at a later time, and expresses that he can make mistakes and should be double-checked: not the typical behavior of a fraud, usually).

replies.author: @christianfeliciano6079
replies.text: @merakimelodies8931no. You have not seen them then. Why did you invent whole videos that I have not seen and went off of that? If you’d like, I’ll give you real videos with real evidence. Not Shad claiming people are making shit up about him.

I wasn’t inventing videos: I named one or two. :) I was just saying those are the ones I’ve personally seen, just to start up the conversation and show where I was coming from, is all. :)

Hmmm, I’m not aware of any scandals or conflicts around the Mario movie of any significance: I know Shad said he didn’t care for it but that it was only mild dislike, while Nate as cohost loved the movie and thought it rocked—so not a huge upset, to the best of my awareness—did I miss something big?

author: @william_hartman
text: Uechi-ryu (Okinawan Karate) teaches these two methods when facing a sword wielder. 1) Run away 2) If you can't run away, keep your distance to just outside their thrust/cut range and if they get out of position close the distance inside their sword's range. We also teach this: if you are facing someone with a knife (or sword) you WILL get cut, but with training you can reduce the severity.

author: @macnadoodle
text:
Decades ago, I used to teach Jiu Jitsu, which included sword defence techniques. One Saturday, we were hosting a visiting club, and decided to make some quick demos, just for fun. I paired up with one bluff Yorkshireman wanted to try out this move, which I said "is utter bullshit!" Essentially, as I was coming down vertically with the sharp Katana (yes we did train with real weapons back then), he was to step in UNDER the arc of the blade and grab my wrists, then throwing me in a kind of Uki Goshi move. We worked on this, slowly at first but getting faster and faster, til the Yorkshirman says "a'right lad, lets go fer it, yabastid!"  So with my best Devonshire swing I come down with a full power cut as he - brave tyke that he was - grabs the blade, not my wrists!
Instinctively, I bounce the blade back up out of his hands while just shouts Aaargh! Concerned, I ask him if he's ok. "Fine", says he, with nothing but a pink line across the base of his left thumb to his wrist. "I'll just rub it better". "Better let Sensei take a look". There's a tiny bead of blood showing on his palm. Sensei slowly teases the cut open to reveal that I almost cut his thumb clean off! Blood gushes everywhere, and yer man is off to ER with a towel round his fist to absorb the blood. He later became Trans. Not sure whether me nearly cutting his thumb off changed his outlook on life  that much, but I guess we'll never know.

author: @VladGenX
text: I've seen the videos they referenced. As a martial artist myself, that crap only works in Mythbusters slowmo. In a real situation, you need to either move or of the way, or enter in and intercept their hands, not the blade. I have done plenty of knife defenses at full speed and even with years of dedication and practice, I still end up getting "cut" once Ina while. It's inevitable. Best defense for this is to truly not be in the situation to begin with

author: @tommeakin1732
text: From what I've seen, countering thrusts is maybe one of the most plausible things you can do without a weapon. Like using the hand and arm to redirect thrusts is a thing against rapiers - something I have seen done in sparring a few times. Against cuts, you're going to get hit. This has no grounding in anything beyond my own thinking, but I'm of the thinking that, if you have no weapon and were to get attacked by a cutting blade, your best bet would be to pretty much throw your weak arm up to just take the blow, in the hope that you can then do something useful with the rest of your body when you're in grappling range. Like somethings going to get hit - you might as well choose what's going to get hit.

author: @bjrnhalfhand2258
text:
You've identified an issue that most traditional martial arts have.
EDIT: Apparently people are missing my point. 
Traditional martial arts are full of techniques and training processes that rely on a compliant partner.
Karate, Aikido, TKD and many others, do the same thing as this video, techniques that require your partner to attack in a very specific way, to react in a specific way.
That little exercise where you partner up with another student and run through your routine. 
They always punch or kick in the same way, you do your little spin or sidestep or whatever and hit back. Your partner just stands there whilst you do all this. 
Aikido in particular is egregious.
Only works in your dojo. Only works before your partner helped you.
That's my point, traditional martial arts are full of that stuff.
I'm not saying it's all they do, not all the training processes they have, but they all do it to some degree.

author: @jamesm2577
text: Stormlight archive has a character do the stupid suicidal "last clap" during a duel that should have just been an execution given the character's opponent had a magic weapon that cuts through anything (including the spear he arrived with).  It worked there because the character knew he was dead & had no other options but to risk it all on something the character himself figured was going to just end with him being sliced in half

author: @matinnesbitt7930
text: the GRAB the sword thing always anointed me, like you can grip the sharpened end in any way to effect the guy holding the handle.

replies.author: @balazskertesz5010
replies.text: Well, you kinda can, as long as the sword is not moving, but only for a  moment. There are disarming techniques like that in sword treatises, but they always assume that you first block the attack with your own sword, and _then_ yank it out from the opponent's hand. Even then, it is a very risky move.
